text:
PET-CT WITH 18F-FDG, WHOLE BODY
SURVEILLANCE IMAGING — 24-MONTH POST-TREATMENT CHECKPOINT

INDICATION:
Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 54-year-old female with a history of HPV-positive (p16+), high-risk HPV type 16, squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue, staged T2N1M0 at diagnosis (AJCC 8th edition conventional staging; stage I by HPV-positive oropharyngeal staging criteria). She was treated with definitive concurrent chemoradiation — intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) using a simultaneous integrated boost technique to 70 Gy at the high-risk target volume, delivered over 35 fractions, with concurrent weekly Platinol (cumulative dose 414 mg/m2; 83% of planned dose delivered). Treatment was completed approximately 24 months prior to this examination. She achieved a complete metabolic response on post-treatment PET-CT performed at approximately 10 weeks post-chemoradiation, which was confirmed by direct laryngoscopy and biopsy demonstrating complete pathologic response (no viable carcinoma). Subsequent surveillance imaging at 9 months demonstrated no evidence of recurrent disease, and MRI neck at 18 months was similarly reassuring with stable post-treatment fibrotic changes at the primary site. This examination is being performed as a 24-month surveillance PET-CT per institutional head and neck oncology surveillance protocol for HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma.

Never-smoker. Known late treatment effects include grade 1 xerostomia and mild high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss. Clinically doing well, ECOG performance status 0.

TECHNIQUE:
Whole-body PET-CT was performed following intravenous administration of 10.8 mCi (399 MBq) of 18F-FDG. The patient fasted for greater than 6 hours prior to injection. Pre-injection serum glucose was 91 mg/dL. Uptake phase was 62 minutes. Diagnostic-quality CT was acquired from the vertex of the skull through the mid-thighs with intravenous iodinated contrast (Omnipaque 350, 100 mL at 3 mL/sec). Images were reconstructed using time-of-flight PET with iterative reconstruction and reviewed in attenuation-corrected and non-attenuation-corrected formats. SUV calculations utilize lean body mass (SUVlbm) and maximum pixel SUV (SUVmax). A low-dose CT component was also available for attenuation correction and anatomic coregistration.

---

FINDINGS:

HEAD AND NECK — PRIMARY SITE:
Right base of tongue: There is no focal area of abnormal FDG hypermetabolism at the right base of tongue. Mild, diffuse mucosal FDG uptake is present bilaterally throughout the oropharyngeal mucosa, with SUVmax 1.7 at the right base of tongue, well within the range expected for normal to mildly inflamed pharyngeal mucosa in the post-radiation setting and indistinguishable from background physiologic activity. There is no discrete mass-like focus of intense or asymmetric tracer avidity to suggest viable tumor. The previously documented residual soft tissue thickening at the right base of tongue, which measured 0.9 cm immediately post-treatment and had decreased to 0.5 cm on MRI at the 18-month surveillance examination, is not discernibly FDG-avid on this study and is consistent with post-treatment fibrosis. No abnormal mucosal uptake is identified in the oral tongue, soft palate, hard palate, tonsillar fossa bilaterally, posterior pharyngeal wall, vallecula, or hypopharynx.

CERVICAL LYMPH NODES:
Right neck: A small fibrotic-appearing node is again present in right level IIA, measuring approximately 0.8 cm in short axis, marginally smaller than on the prior 18-month MRI (0.7 cm, within measurement variability). No pathologic FDG uptake is associated with this node; SUVmax is 1.5, which does not exceed mediastinal blood pool activity. No internal necrosis, no loss of fatty hilum appreciable on CT component. This node has remained stable in appearance across multiple prior imaging studies and its appearance is entirely consistent with a post-treatment reactive or fibrotic node. No abnormal FDG-avid lymphadenopathy identified elsewhere in the right neck (levels I, IIB, III, IV, V, VI all unremarkable).

Left neck: No pathologically enlarged or FDG-avid lymph nodes. Left-sided cervical chain lymph nodes are small and within normal limits. No left retropharyngeal, left submandibular, or left supraclavicular adenopathy.

Bilateral retropharyngeal space: No pathologic adenopathy. No abnormal FDG-avid focus.

Salivary glands: Bilateral parotid and submandibular glands demonstrate symmetric, mildly reduced FDG activity compared to expected normal physiologic glandular uptake, consistent with post-radiation salivary gland atrophy, particularly on the right. This is a well-recognized post-treatment change and does not suggest neoplastic involvement. No parotid mass or discrete nodule identified.

Thyroid gland: The thyroid is symmetric in size and morphology. No focal FDG-avid thyroid nodule or mass identified. There is mild, homogeneous, low-level thyroid uptake bilaterally (SUVmax 1.4), within physiologic limits. CT component: no discrete thyroid nodule or calcification identified. This is notable given the patient's history of neck irradiation and clinical monitoring for radiation-induced thyroid abnormality; no concerning findings at this time.

Mandible and skull base: No FDG-avid foci within the mandible, skull base, or calvarium to suggest osseous involvement or osteoradionecrosis. The mandible demonstrates no cortical destruction or lytic lesion on CT component.

Soft tissues of the neck: Post-radiation fibrotic changes are present in the right neck soft tissues, with mild thickening of the platysma and right sternocleidomastoid muscle compared to the contralateral side, consistent with expected radiation-field changes. No abnormal FDG uptake within the neck soft tissues. No subcutaneous mass or lymphocele. No evidence of radiation-induced carotid artery plaque with FDG avidity (no FDG-avid vascular wall activity to suggest florid inflammatory atheromatous plaque, though this is an insensitive modality for carotid plaque evaluation).

THORAX:
Lungs and pleura: The lungs are well-aerated bilaterally. No pulmonary nodule, mass, or consolidation identified. No pleural effusion. No pleural-based FDG-avid focus. No endobronchial lesion.

Mediastinum and hila: No pathologically enlarged or FDG-avid mediastinal lymph nodes. Mediastinal contours are unremarkable. No paratracheal, subcarinal, or aortopulmonary window adenopathy. No hilar lymphadenopathy bilaterally.

Heart and pericardium: No pericardial effusion. No FDG-avid cardiac or pericardial focus.

Supraclavicular regions: No FDG-avid supraclavicular adenopathy bilaterally.

ABDOMEN AND PELVIS:
Liver: The liver is normal in size with a smooth capsular contour. No focal hepatic lesion. No FDG-avid focus within the liver parenchyma. No biliary ductal dilatation. Portal venous structures unremarkable.

Spleen: Normal size and morphology. No FDG-avid splenic lesion. Spleen measures 10.2 cm in craniocaudal dimension.

Pancreas: No FDG-avid pancreatic lesion. The pancreatic duct is not dilated on CT.

Adrenal glands: Bilateral adrenal glands appear normal without FDG-avid mass or nodule. No adrenal enlargement.

Kidneys and collecting system: Normal bilateral renal parenchymal uptake. Physiologic bilateral renal pelvicalyceal and ureteral FDG activity from urinary excretion. No renal mass or hydronephrosis on CT. No perinephric abnormality.

Gastrointestinal tract: Physiologic FDG activity throughout the bowel. No abnormal focal bowel activity to suggest primary bowel neoplasm or serosal implant. No evidence of peritoneal carcinomatosis. No ascites.

Pelvis: No pathologic pelvic adenopathy. No FDG-avid pelvic mass. Uterus and adnexa are within normal limits for age. Urinary bladder contains the expected high-activity urine pool from 18F-FDG excretion; no intraluminal or mural bladder lesion appreciable.

Retroperitoneum: No retroperitoneal or para-aortic lymphadenopathy. Aorta is normal caliber.

BONES AND SOFT TISSUES (WHOLE BODY):
No FDG-avid osseous lesion to suggest bone metastasis. The axial skeleton, pelvis, and visualized long bones all demonstrate normal marrow uptake for age. No lytic or sclerotic lesion on CT component. No pathologic fracture. No soft tissue mass.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. No evidence of FDG-avid locoregional recurrent disease at the right base of tongue primary site. SUVmax 1.7 at the right base of tongue is well within physiologic range and indistinguishable from the post-treatment/post-radiation mucosal changes observed on all prior surveillance studies. No progression since last PET-CT at 9 months post-treatment.

2. The small right level IIA lymph node previously documented is stable in size and morphology and shows no meaningful FDG uptake. Its appearance is consistent with treated/fibrotic post-treatment nodal tissue. No new or enlarging cervical adenopathy bilaterally.

3. No distant metastatic disease identified. Thorax, abdomen, and pelvis are without FDG-avid or CT-correlate lesion to suggest systemic spread.

4. Thyroid gland appears unremarkable without FDG-avid nodularity, though clinical surveillance per irradiated field protocol is ongoing and thyroid ultrasound remains the preferred modality for thyroid nodule evaluation.

5. Post-radiation changes in the right neck soft tissues and salivary glands are stable and expected at 24 months post-completion of IMRT.

Overall, this is a highly reassuring 24-month surveillance examination with no imaging evidence of recurrent or metastatic HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma.
